# Acceptance Criteria: Task Status Ownership

## US-1: Remove server-side in_progress on assignment

```gherkin
Feature: Agent session creation does not change task status

  Scenario: Creating agent session with a ready task
    Given a task with status "ready"
    When an agent session is created for that task
    Then the task status remains "ready"
    And the task source_session is linked to the new session

  Scenario: Creating agent session with a todo task
    Given a task with status "todo"
    When an agent session is created for that task
    Then the task status remains "todo"
    And the task source_session is linked to the new session
```

## US-2: Remove server-side done on session accept

```gherkin
Feature: Session accept does not change task status

  Scenario: Accepting a session with task in in_progress
    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "in_progress"
    When the session is accepted
    Then the session status becomes "accepted"
    And the task status remains "in_progress"

  Scenario: Accepting a session with task already done
    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "done"
    When the session is accepted
    Then the session status becomes "accepted"
    And the task status remains "done"
```

## US-3: Add brain commit-check CLI command

```gherkin
Feature: brain commit-check parses task refs and sets done

  Scenario: Commit message with single task ref
    Given a commit with message "Implement login flow\n\ntask:abc123"
    When brain commit-check runs
    Then task "abc123" status is set to "done"

  Scenario: Commit message with multiple task refs
    Given a commit with message "Batch update\n\ntasks: abc123, def456"
    When brain commit-check runs
    Then task "abc123" status is set to "done"
    And task "def456" status is set to "done"

  Scenario: Commit message with no task refs
    Given a commit with message "Fix typo in README"
    When brain commit-check runs
    Then no task status changes occur

  Scenario: Task already done (idempotent)
    Given a commit referencing task "abc123"
    And task "abc123" already has status "done"
    When brain commit-check runs
    Then task "abc123" status remains "done"
    And no error is raised
```

## US-4: Wire commit-check as post-commit hook

```gherkin
Feature: commit-check runs as post-commit hook

  Scenario: Git commit triggers commit-check
    Given a Brain-managed repository with post-commit hook installed
    When a developer makes a git commit with message containing "task:abc123"
    Then brain commit-check runs automatically after the commit
    And task "abc123" status is set to "done"
```

## US-5: GitHub processor sets done on push

```gherkin
Feature: GitHub commit processor sets done on push to non-main branch

  Scenario: Push to feature branch with task refs
    Given a commit pushed to branch "feature/login" with message "task:abc123"
    When the GitHub commit processor runs
    Then task "abc123" status is set to "done"
    And an implemented_by relation is created

  Scenario: Push to main branch
    Given a commit pushed to branch "main" with message "task:abc123"
    When the GitHub commit processor runs
    Then task "abc123" status is set to "completed"
    And an implemented_by relation is created
```

## US-6: GitHub processor sets completed on merge to main

```gherkin
Feature: GitHub commit processor sets completed on merge

  Scenario: PR merged to main with task refs
    Given commits merged to "main" referencing "task:abc123"
    When the GitHub commit processor runs for the merge
    Then task "abc123" status is set to "completed"

  Scenario: Task already completed (idempotent)
    Given a merge to main referencing "task:abc123"
    And task "abc123" already has status "completed"
    When the GitHub commit processor runs
    Then task "abc123" status remains "completed"
```

## Backward transitions (unchanged, for reference)

```gherkin
Feature: Server owns backward transitions

  Scenario: Session aborted resets task to ready
    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "in_progress"
    When the session is aborted
    Then the task status is set to "ready"

  Scenario: Session rejected resets task to ready
    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "in_progress"
    When the session is rejected
    Then the task status is set to "ready"
```

file.content:
# Requirements: Task Status Ownership

## Problem Statement

Task status transitions are duplicated between the server (orchestrator) and agents/processors. The server optimistically sets in_progress on assignment and done on session accept, but these are redundant with the agent's brain-start-task command and the GitHub commit processor. This creates confusion about who owns the truth and causes orphaned states on agent crashes.

## Design Principle

**Forward transitions are agent/processor-owned. Backward transitions are server-owned.**

The entity doing the work owns progress signals. The orchestrator owns recovery.

| Transition | Owner | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| → in_progress | Agent | brain-start-task command |
| → done | commit-check hook (local) / GitHub processor (remote) | git commit with task refs |
| → completed | GitHub processor | merge to main |
| → ready (abort) | Server | Session aborted |
| → ready (reject) | Server | Session rejected |

## Requirements

### R1: Remove server-side forward transitions

Remove the in_progress transition from `createAgentSession()` in `mcp-queries.ts`. The server should still link `source_session` to the task but not change status.

Remove the done transition from `acceptOrchestratorSession()` in `session-lifecycle.ts`. Session accept is a review gate, not a completion event.

### R2: Agent owns in_progress

`brain-start-task` already instructs the agent to call `update_task_status` → in_progress. This becomes the sole authority. No changes needed here.

### R3: Add `brain commit-check` CLI command

New CLI command that:
1. Reads the most recent commit message (or accepts a commit SHA)
2. Parses task refs using existing `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts`
3. Calls the Brain API to set each referenced task's status → done
4. Idempotent — setting done on an already-done task is a no-op

### R4: Wire commit-check as git post-commit hook

Install `brain commit-check` as a git post-commit hook, similar to how Brain already manages pre-commit hooks. The hook calls the Brain HTTP API.

### R5: GitHub commit processor sets done on push

Extend `github-commit-processor.ts` to set task status → done (in addition to creating `implemented_by` relations) when commits are pushed to non-main branches.

### R6: GitHub commit processor sets completed on merge to main

When commits land on the main/default branch (via merge), set referenced tasks → completed. This is the "verified/merged" signal for teams.

### R7: Keep server-owned backward transitions

Abort and reject continue to reset task status on the server side:
- Abort → ready
- Reject → ready

No changes needed here.

## Setup-Dependent Behavior

| Setup | in_progress | done | completed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solo/local | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check post-commit hook | N/A or manual |
| Team/remote | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check + GitHub processor | GitHub processor (merge to main) |

## Non-Requirements

- No migration of existing task statuses. Schema & data migration policy: breaking changes, no backfills.
- No new task status values. Existing statuses (open, todo, ready, in_progress, blocked, done, completed) are sufficient.

input.new_string:
## Constraints

- **commit-check must not block git**: The post-commit hook must never fail the git workflow. API errors are logged but exit 0. This is a fire-and-forget signal.
- **Idempotent transitions**: Setting done on an already-done task, or completed on an already-completed task, is a no-op. Both commit-check and GitHub processor may fire for the same commit — that's fine.
- **Task ref convention required**: Completion detection depends on `task:<id>` or `tasks: <id1>, <id2>` in commit messages. No task refs = no status change. This is by design — not every commit maps to a task.

## Non-Requirements

# Requirements: Task Status Ownership

## Problem Statement

Task status transitions are duplicated between the server (orchestrator) and agents/processors. The server optimistically sets in_progress on assignment and done on session accept, but these are redundant with the agent's brain-start-task command and the GitHub commit processor. This creates confusion about who owns the truth and causes orphaned states on agent crashes.

## Design Principle

**Forward transitions are agent/processor-owned. Backward transitions are server-owned.**

The entity doing the work owns progress signals. The orchestrator owns recovery.

| Transition | Owner | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| → in_progress | Agent | brain-start-task command |
| → done | commit-check hook (local) / GitHub processor (remote) | git commit with task refs |
| → completed | GitHub processor | merge to main |
| → ready (abort) | Server | Session aborted |
| → ready (reject) | Server | Session rejected |

## Requirements

### R1: Remove server-side forward transitions

Remove the in_progress transition from `createAgentSession()` in `mcp-queries.ts`. The server should still link `source_session` to the task but not change status.

Remove the done transition from `acceptOrchestratorSession()` in `session-lifecycle.ts`. Session accept is a review gate, not a completion event.

### R2: Agent owns in_progress

`brain-start-task` already instructs the agent to call `update_task_status` → in_progress. This becomes the sole authority. No changes needed here.

### R3: Add `brain commit-check` CLI command

New CLI command that:
1. Reads the most recent commit message (or accepts a commit SHA)
2. Parses task refs using existing `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts`
3. Calls the Brain API to set each referenced task's status → done
4. Idempotent — setting done on an already-done task is a no-op

### R4: Wire commit-check as git post-commit hook

Install `brain commit-check` as a git post-commit hook, similar to how Brain already manages pre-commit hooks. The hook calls the Brain HTTP API.

### R5: GitHub commit processor sets done on push

Extend `github-commit-processor.ts` to set task status → done (in addition to creating `implemented_by` relations) when commits are pushed to non-main branches.

### R6: GitHub commit processor sets completed on merge to main

When commits land on the main/default branch (via merge), set referenced tasks → completed. This is the "verified/merged" signal for teams.

### R7: Keep server-owned backward transitions

Abort and reject continue to reset task status on the server side:
- Abort → ready
- Reject → ready

No changes needed here.

## Setup-Dependent Behavior

| Setup | in_progress | done | completed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solo/local | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check post-commit hook | N/A or manual |
| Team/remote | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check + GitHub processor | GitHub processor (merge to main) |

## Non-Requirements

- No migration of existing task statuses. Schema & data migration policy: breaking changes, no backfills.
- No new task status values. Existing statuses (open, todo, ready, in_progress, blocked, done, completed) are sufficient.

content.text:
DISCUSS wave artifacts are complete and ready for DESIGN handoff:

**Artifacts created:**
- `docs/ux/task-status-ownership/` — JTBD job stories, four forces, opportunity scores
- `docs/requirements/task-status-ownership/` — requirements, user stories, acceptance criteria, DoR checklist

**Summary of the design:**
- **Forward transitions** (in_progress, done, completed) owned by agents and commit processors
- **Backward transitions** (abort → ready, reject → ready) owned by the server
- **6 user stories**: 2 removals (S), 2 new CLI (M+S), 2 GitHub processor enhancements (S+M)
- **Implementation order**: US-1+2 first (remove server transitions), then US-3+4 (commit-check) and US-5+6 (GitHub processor) in parallel

Ready to proceed to `/nw:design` when you are.

1→---
     2→name: review-criteria
     3→description: Review dimensions and bug patterns for journey artifact reviews
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Review Criteria Skill
     7→
     8→Domain knowledge for product-owner-reviewer (Eclipse). Covers journey coherence, emotional arcs, shared artifacts, example data quality, CLI UX patterns.
     9→
    10→## Review Dimensions
    11→
    12→### Journey Coherence
    13→Validate complete flow with no gaps.
    14→
    15→Checks: all steps start-to-goal defined | no orphan steps | no dead ends | decision branches lead somewhere | error paths guide to recovery
    16→
    17→Severity: critical = missing main flow steps / dead ends | high = orphan steps | medium = ambiguous decisions | low = minor clarity
    18→
    19→### Emotional Arc
    20→Validate emotional design quality.
    21→
    22→Checks: arc defined (start/middle/end) | all steps annotated | no jarring transitions | confidence builds progressively | error states guide not frustrate
    23→
    24→Severity: critical = no arc / major jarring transitions | high = missing key annotations | medium = confidence doesn't build | low = minor polish
    25→
    26→### Shared Artifact Tracking
    27→Validate ${variable} sources and consistency.
    28→
    29→Checks: all ${variables} have documented source | single source of truth | all consumers listed | integration risks assessed | validation methods specified
    30→
    31→Severity: critical = undocumented ${variables} / multiple sources | high = missing consumers / unassessed risks | medium = incomplete validation | low = minor consumer docs
    32→
    33→### Example Data Quality
    34→Key review skill -- analyze data for integration gaps.
    35→
    36→Checks: realistic not generic | reveals integration dependencies | catches version mismatches | catches path inconsistencies | consistent across steps
    37→
    38→Severity: critical = generic placeholders hide issues | high = inconsistent across steps | medium = doesn't reveal deps | low = could be more realistic
    39→
    40→Apply: 1) trace ${version} through all steps -- same? 2) compare ${install_path} step 2 vs 3 -- match? 3) does data show actual integration points?
    41→
    42→Generic "v1.0.0" or "/path/to/install" hides bugs. Realistic "v1.2.86" from "pyproject.toml" reveals bugs.
    43→
    44→### CLI UX Patterns
    45→Checks: command vocabulary consistent | help available | error messages guide to resolution | progressive disclosure respected
    46→
    47→Severity: critical = inconsistent commands | high = no error recovery guidance | medium = missing progressive disclosure | low = minor vocabulary
    48→
    49→## Four Bug Patterns
    50→
    51→### Pattern 1: Version Mismatch
    52→Multiple version sources. Trace ${version} through all steps -- same source?
    53→```
    54→Step 1: v${version} from pyproject.toml
    55→Step 2: v${version} from version.txt  <-- MISMATCH
    56→```
    57→
    58→### Pattern 2: Hardcoded URLs
    59→URLs without canonical source. For each URL: "where is this defined?"
    60→```
    61→Install: git+https://github.com/org/repo
    62→<-- Where is this URL canonically defined?
    63→```
    64→
    65→### Pattern 3: Path Inconsistency
    66→Paths from different sources. Trace ${path} -- same source?
    67→```
    68→Install to: ${install_path} from config
    69→Uninstall from: ~/.claude/agents/nw/  <-- HARDCODED
    70→```
    71→
    72→### Pattern 4: Missing Commands
    73→CLI commands without slash equivalents. Check both contexts exist.
    74→```
    75→Terminal: crafter run
    76→Claude Code: /nw:execute  <-- EXISTS?
    77→```
    78→
    79→## Review Output Schema
    80→
    81→```yaml
    82→review_id: "{timestamp}"
    83→reviewer: "nw-product-owner-reviewer (Eclipse)"
    84→artifact_reviewed: "{file path}"
    85→
    86→strengths:
    87→  - strength: "{Positive aspect}"
    88→    example: "{Specific evidence}"
    89→
    90→issues_identified:
    91→  journey_coherence:
    92→    - issue: "{Description}"
    93→      severity: "critical|high|medium|low"
    94→      location: "{Where}"
    95→      recommendation: "{Fix}"
    96→  emotional_arc:
    97→    - issue: "{Description}"
    98→      severity: "critical|high|medium|low"
    99→      location: "{Where}"
   100→      recommendation: "{Fix}"
   101→  shared_artifacts:
   102→    - issue: "{Description}"
   103→      severity: "critical|high|medium|low"
   104→      artifact: "{Which ${variable}}"
   105→      recommendation: "{Fix}"
   106→  example_data:
   107→    - issue: "{Description}"
   108→      severity: "critical|high|medium|low"
   109→      data_point: "{Which data}"
   110→      integration_risk: "{What bug it might hide}"
   111→      recommendation: "{Fix}"
   112→  bug_patterns_detected:
   113→    - pattern: "version_mismatch|hardcoded_url|path_inconsistency|missing_command"
   114→      severity: "critical|high"
   115→      evidence: "{Finding}"
   116→      recommendation: "{Fix}"
   117→
   118→recommendations:
   119→  critical: ["{Must fix before approval}"]
   120→  high: ["{Should fix before approval}"]
   121→  medium: ["{Fix in next iteration}"]
   122→  low: ["{Consider for polish}"]
   123→
   124→approval_status: "approved|rejected_pending_revisions|conditionally_approved"
   125→approval_conditions: "{If conditional, what must be done}"
   126→```
   127→
   128→## Approval Criteria
   129→
   130→- **approved**: No critical, no high issues
   131→- **conditionally_approved**: No critical, some high addressable quickly
   132→- **rejected_pending_revisions**: Critical issues exist, or multiple high
   133→

1→---
     2→name: dor-validation
     3→description: Definition of Ready checklist criteria, antipattern detection patterns, UAT quality rules, and domain language enforcement for product owner review
     4→---
     5→
     6→# DoR Validation and Antipattern Detection
     7→
     8→## Definition of Ready Checklist (8 Items - Hard Gate)
     9→
    10→All items must PASS with evidence. Each FAIL blocks handoff.
    11→
    12→### Item 1: Problem Statement Clear and Validated
    13→Domain language (not technical), describes real user pain, testable.
    14→Pass: "Maria wastes 30 seconds typing credentials on every visit"
    15→Fail: "Users need authentication" | "Implement login feature"
    16→
    17→### Item 2: User/Persona with Specific Characteristics
    18→Real name, specific role, clear context.
    19→Pass: "Maria Santos, returning customer (2+ orders), using trusted MacBook"
    20→Fail: "User" | "Customer" | "End user" | "Authenticated user"
    21→
    22→### Item 3: At Least 3 Domain Examples with Real Data
    23→Min 3 examples, real names (not user123), real values, different scenarios (happy/edge/error).
    24→Pass: "Example 1: Maria on MacBook, 5 days since login, goes to dashboard"
    25→Fail: "User logs in successfully" | "Test with valid credentials"
    26→
    27→### Item 4: UAT Scenarios Cover Happy Path + Edge Cases
    28→Given/When/Then format, 3-7 scenarios, real data, covers happy + edge.
    29→Pass: "Given Maria authenticated on 'MacBook-Home' 5 days ago..."
    30→Fail: "Test login works" | "Given a user When they login Then success"
    31→
    32→### Item 5: Acceptance Criteria Derived from UAT
    33→Checkable (checkbox), traceable to UAT, outcome-focused (not implementation).
    34→Pass: "Sessions older than 30 days require re-authentication"
    35→Fail: "Use JWT tokens" | "System should work correctly"
    36→
    37→### Item 6: Story Right-Sized (1-3 Days, 3-7 Scenarios)
    38→Effort estimate provided, scenario count in range, single demonstrable outcome.
    39→Pass: 2 days, 5 UAT scenarios, demoed in single session
    40→Fail: >7 scenarios | >3 days | multiple distinct outcomes
    41→
    42→### Item 7: Technical Notes Identify Constraints
    43→Dependencies listed, risks identified, architectural considerations noted.
    44→Pass: "Requires JWT token storage, GDPR cookie consent integration"
    45→Fail: no technical notes section
    46→
    47→### Item 8: Dependencies Resolved or Tracked
    48→Blocking deps identified, resolution status clear, escalation path.
    49→Pass: "Depends on US-041 (completed) and Auth service API (available)"
    50→Fail: "Needs some API - TBD"
    51→
    52→---
    53→
    54→## Antipattern Detection (8 Patterns)
    55→
    56→### 1. Implement-X (critical)
    57→Signal: starts with "Implement", "Add", "Create", "Build", "Develop"
    58→Detection: `^(Implement|Add|Create|Build|Develop)\s` | Fix: rewrite as user pain
    59→
    60→### 2. Generic Data (high)
    61→Signal: user123, test@test.com, foo, bar, lorem, placeholder
    62→Detection: `user[0-9]+`, `test@`, `example@`, `foo`, `bar` | Fix: real names -- Maria Santos
    63→
    64→### 3. Technical AC (high)
    65→Signal: AC describes implementation not outcome
    66→Detection: "Use JWT", "Implement using", "Database should", "API must return"
    67→Fix: outcome focus -- "Session persists for 30 days"
    68→
    69→### 4. Giant Stories (critical)
    70→Signal: >7 scenarios | >3 days | multiple distinct outcomes | Fix: split by user outcome
    71→
    72→### 5. No Examples (critical)
    73→Signal: no "Example" section | <3 examples | abstract examples | Fix: add 3+ with real data
    74→
    75→### 6. Tests After Code (high)
    76→Signal: "Tests to be added", "Will write tests later", "Tests TBD" | Fix: UAT first, RED first
    77→
    78→### 7. Vague Persona (high)
    79→Signal: "User", "Customer", "End user" as persona | Fix: "Maria Santos, returning customer (2+ orders)"
    80→
    81→### 8. Missing Edge Cases (medium)
    82→Signal: all success scenarios, no errors, no boundaries | Fix: add expired session, invalid device, etc.
    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→## UAT Scenario Quality Checks
    87→
    88→**Format**: Given/When/Then with complete sentences. Fail: "Test login", "Given user When login Then success"
    89→**Real Data**: real names, values, scenarios. Fail: "Given user123", "When X happens"
    90→**Coverage**: min 1 happy path + 1 edge + 1 error. Range: 3-7 scenarios.
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## Domain Language Checks
    95→
    96→**Technical Jargon**: flag in user-facing sections: JWT, API, database, backend, frontend, microservice, REST, HTTP, JSON, SQL. Exception: Technical Notes section. Fix: "session token" -> "remember me"
    97→
    98→**Generic Language**: flag "the system", "the application", "functionality", "feature". Fix: use specific names -- "the login page" -> "the welcome screen"
    99→

1→# JTBD Job Stories: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→## Job 1: Accurate Task Progress (Solo Developer)
     4→
     5→**Job Story**: When I'm working solo on tasks using Claude Code locally, I want task status to reflect what's actually happening (agent started, code committed), so I can trust the task board without manually updating statuses.
     6→
     7→### Dimensions
     8→- **Functional**: Task status updates automatically based on real events (agent start, commit)
     9→- **Emotional**: Confidence that the board reflects reality without babysitting
    10→- **Social**: N/A (solo context)
    11→
    12→## Job 2: Reliable Task Tracking (Team Lead)
    13→
    14→**Job Story**: When my team has multiple agents working on tasks across branches, I want task completion to be tied to actual code landing in main, so I can trust that "completed" means verified and merged — not just that an agent said it finished.
    15→
    16→### Dimensions
    17→- **Functional**: Merge-to-main triggers completed status; push triggers done (pending review)
    18→- **Emotional**: Trust that status reflects shipped reality, not agent optimism
    19→- **Social**: Team can rely on task board as source of truth in standups
    20→
    21→## Job 3: Graceful Recovery (Orchestrator Operator)
    22→
    23→**Job Story**: When an agent crashes or produces rejected work, I want the task to automatically become available again, so I don't have stuck tasks blocking the pipeline.
    24→
    25→### Dimensions
    26→- **Functional**: Abort/reject resets task to "ready" so another agent can pick it up
    27→- **Emotional**: No anxiety about orphaned in-progress tasks
    28→- **Social**: Team doesn't see phantom "in progress" work
    29→

1→# Four Forces Analysis: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→## Job 1: Accurate Task Progress (Solo Developer)
     4→
     5→| Force | Description |
     6→|-------|-------------|
     7→| **Push** (frustration) | Server sets in_progress on assignment AND agent sets it again via brain-start-task — redundant. Server sets done on session accept, but commit is the real completion event. Status doesn't reflect reality. |
     8→| **Pull** (desired future) | Task status updates driven by real events: agent actually starting work, code actually being committed. Single source of truth per transition. |
     9→| **Anxiety** (adoption concern) | What if agent crashes before setting in_progress? Task stays in ready — but that's actually correct (no work started). |
    10→| **Habit** (current behavior) | Relying on server to optimistically set status. Must trust that agents and hooks will do their job instead. |
    11→
    12→## Job 2: Reliable Task Tracking (Team Lead)
    13→
    14→| Force | Description |
    15→|-------|-------------|
    16→| **Push** | Session accept sets done, but code might not be merged yet. GitHub commit processor only creates relations, doesn't update status. No distinction between "work finished" and "verified/merged". |
    17→| **Pull** | Two-phase completion: done (committed, pending review) and completed (merged to main). GitHub processor as remote authority for teams. |
    18→| **Anxiety** | What if commit message doesn't include task refs? Task stays in_progress. Need convention enforcement. |
    19→| **Habit** | Treating session accept as task completion. Must shift to commit-as-completion mental model. |
    20→
    21→## Job 3: Graceful Recovery (Orchestrator Operator)
    22→
    23→| Force | Description |
    24→|-------|-------------|
    25→| **Push** | If agent crashes after server set in_progress, task is stuck — no agent working on it, but status says otherwise. |
    26→| **Pull** | Server only handles backward transitions (abort/reject → ready). Forward transitions owned by the entity doing the work. Clean separation. |
    27→| **Anxiety** | None significant — server already handles abort/reject. This is the one area that stays server-owned. |
    28→| **Habit** | Server owning all transitions. Must accept split ownership model. |
    29→

1→# Opportunity Scores: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→| Job | Importance (1-10) | Current Satisfaction (1-10) | Opportunity Score | Priority |
     4→|-----|-------------------|----------------------------|-------------------|----------|
     5→| Accurate Task Progress (Solo) | 8 | 4 | 12 | High |
     6→| Reliable Task Tracking (Team) | 9 | 3 | 15 | Highest |
     7→| Graceful Recovery (Orchestrator) | 7 | 6 | 8 | Medium |
     8→
     9→**Opportunity Score** = Importance + (Importance - Satisfaction)
    10→
    11→## Analysis
    12→
    13→- **Reliable Task Tracking** scores highest because teams currently have no distinction between "agent says done" and "code merged" — the commit processor doesn't touch status at all.
    14→- **Accurate Task Progress** is high because the duplicate in_progress transition is actively confusing and the server's optimistic set creates orphaned states on crashes.
    15→- **Graceful Recovery** scores lowest because abort/reject already work — they just need to stay server-owned rather than being removed.
    16→

1→# Requirements: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→## Problem Statement
     4→
     5→Task status transitions are duplicated between the server (orchestrator) and agents/processors. The server optimistically sets in_progress on assignment and done on session accept, but these are redundant with the agent's brain-start-task command and the GitHub commit processor. This creates confusion about who owns the truth and causes orphaned states on agent crashes.
     6→
     7→## Design Principle
     8→
     9→**Forward transitions are agent/processor-owned. Backward transitions are server-owned.**
    10→
    11→The entity doing the work owns progress signals. The orchestrator owns recovery.
    12→
    13→| Transition | Owner | Trigger |
    14→|---|---|---|
    15→| → in_progress | Agent | brain-start-task command |
    16→| → done | commit-check hook (local) / GitHub processor (remote) | git commit with task refs |
    17→| → completed | GitHub processor | merge to main |
    18→| → ready (abort) | Server | Session aborted |
    19→| → ready (reject) | Server | Session rejected |
    20→
    21→## Requirements
    22→
    23→### R1: Remove server-side forward transitions
    24→
    25→Remove the in_progress transition from `createAgentSession()` in `mcp-queries.ts`. The server should still link `source_session` to the task but not change status.
    26→
    27→Remove the done transition from `acceptOrchestratorSession()` in `session-lifecycle.ts`. Session accept is a review gate, not a completion event.
    28→
    29→### R2: Agent owns in_progress
    30→
    31→`brain-start-task` already instructs the agent to call `update_task_status` → in_progress. This becomes the sole authority. No changes needed here.
    32→
    33→### R3: Add `brain commit-check` CLI command
    34→
    35→New CLI command that:
    36→1. Reads the most recent commit message (or accepts a commit SHA)
    37→2. Parses task refs using existing `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts`
    38→3. Calls the Brain API to set each referenced task's status → done
    39→4. Idempotent — setting done on an already-done task is a no-op
    40→
    41→### R4: Wire commit-check as git post-commit hook
    42→
    43→Install `brain commit-check` as a git post-commit hook, similar to how Brain already manages pre-commit hooks. The hook calls the Brain HTTP API.
    44→
    45→### R5: GitHub commit processor sets done on push
    46→
    47→Extend `github-commit-processor.ts` to set task status → done (in addition to creating `implemented_by` relations) when commits are pushed to non-main branches.
    48→
    49→### R6: GitHub commit processor sets completed on merge to main
    50→
    51→When commits land on the main/default branch (via merge), set referenced tasks → completed. This is the "verified/merged" signal for teams.
    52→
    53→### R7: Keep server-owned backward transitions
    54→
    55→Abort and reject continue to reset task status on the server side:
    56→- Abort → ready
    57→- Reject → ready
    58→
    59→No changes needed here.
    60→
    61→## Setup-Dependent Behavior
    62→
    63→| Setup | in_progress | done | completed |
    64→|---|---|---|---|
    65→| Solo/local | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check post-commit hook | N/A or manual |
    66→| Team/remote | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check + GitHub processor | GitHub processor (merge to main) |
    67→
    68→## Non-Requirements
    69→
    70→- No migration of existing task statuses. Schema & data migration policy: breaking changes, no backfills.
    71→- No new task status values. Existing statuses (open, todo, ready, in_progress, blocked, done, completed) are sufficient.
    72→

1→# User Stories: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→## US-1: Remove server-side in_progress on assignment
     4→
     5→**As** a developer using the orchestrator,
     6→**I want** the server to stop setting in_progress when creating an agent session,
     7→**So that** only the agent that actually starts work owns that transition.
     8→
     9→**Traces to**: Job 1 (Accurate Task Progress), Job 3 (Graceful Recovery)
    10→**Size**: S
    11→
    12→## US-2: Remove server-side done on session accept
    13→
    14→**As** a developer reviewing agent work,
    15→**I want** session accept to only affect session lifecycle (not task status),
    16→**So that** task completion is tied to actual code, not review approval.
    17→
    18→**Traces to**: Job 1 (Accurate Task Progress), Job 2 (Reliable Task Tracking)
    19→**Size**: S
    20→
    21→## US-3: Add brain commit-check CLI command
    22→
    23→**As** a solo developer committing code locally,
    24→**I want** a `brain commit-check` command that parses task refs from commit messages and sets tasks to done,
    25→**So that** task status updates automatically when I commit without needing a remote push.
    26→
    27→**Traces to**: Job 1 (Accurate Task Progress)
    28→**Size**: M
    29→
    30→## US-4: Wire commit-check as post-commit hook
    31→
    32→**As** a developer using Brain-managed repositories,
    33→**I want** `brain commit-check` to run automatically as a git post-commit hook,
    34→**So that** I don't have to remember to run it manually after every commit.
    35→
    36→**Traces to**: Job 1 (Accurate Task Progress)
    37→**Size**: S
    38→
    39→## US-5: GitHub processor sets done on push
    40→
    41→**As** a team lead with agents pushing to remote branches,
    42→**I want** the GitHub commit processor to set referenced tasks to done when commits are pushed,
    43→**So that** the team can see which tasks have finished work pending review.
    44→
    45→**Traces to**: Job 2 (Reliable Task Tracking)
    46→**Size**: S
    47→
    48→## US-6: GitHub processor sets completed on merge to main
    49→
    50→**As** a team lead tracking shipped work,
    51→**I want** tasks to be marked completed when their commits merge to main,
    52→**So that** "completed" means the code is verified and landed — not just that an agent finished.
    53→
    54→**Traces to**: Job 2 (Reliable Task Tracking)
    55→**Size**: M
    56→

1→# Acceptance Criteria: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→## US-1: Remove server-side in_progress on assignment
     4→
     5→```gherkin
     6→Feature: Agent session creation does not change task status
     7→
     8→  Scenario: Creating agent session with a ready task
     9→    Given a task with status "ready"
    10→    When an agent session is created for that task
    11→    Then the task status remains "ready"
    12→    And the task source_session is linked to the new session
    13→
    14→  Scenario: Creating agent session with a todo task
    15→    Given a task with status "todo"
    16→    When an agent session is created for that task
    17→    Then the task status remains "todo"
    18→    And the task source_session is linked to the new session
    19→```
    20→
    21→## US-2: Remove server-side done on session accept
    22→
    23→```gherkin
    24→Feature: Session accept does not change task status
    25→
    26→  Scenario: Accepting a session with task in in_progress
    27→    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "in_progress"
    28→    When the session is accepted
    29→    Then the session status becomes "accepted"
    30→    And the task status remains "in_progress"
    31→
    32→  Scenario: Accepting a session with task already done
    33→    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "done"
    34→    When the session is accepted
    35→    Then the session status becomes "accepted"
    36→    And the task status remains "done"
    37→```
    38→
    39→## US-3: Add brain commit-check CLI command
    40→
    41→```gherkin
    42→Feature: brain commit-check parses task refs and sets done
    43→
    44→  Scenario: Commit message with single task ref
    45→    Given a commit with message "Implement login flow\n\ntask:abc123"
    46→    When brain commit-check runs
    47→    Then task "abc123" status is set to "done"
    48→
    49→  Scenario: Commit message with multiple task refs
    50→    Given a commit with message "Batch update\n\ntasks: abc123, def456"
    51→    When brain commit-check runs
    52→    Then task "abc123" status is set to "done"
    53→    And task "def456" status is set to "done"
    54→
    55→  Scenario: Commit message with no task refs
    56→    Given a commit with message "Fix typo in README"
    57→    When brain commit-check runs
    58→    Then no task status changes occur
    59→
    60→  Scenario: Task already done (idempotent)
    61→    Given a commit referencing task "abc123"
    62→    And task "abc123" already has status "done"
    63→    When brain commit-check runs
    64→    Then task "abc123" status remains "done"
    65→    And no error is raised
    66→```
    67→
    68→## US-4: Wire commit-check as post-commit hook
    69→
    70→```gherkin
    71→Feature: commit-check runs as post-commit hook
    72→
    73→  Scenario: Git commit triggers commit-check
    74→    Given a Brain-managed repository with post-commit hook installed
    75→    When a developer makes a git commit with message containing "task:abc123"
    76→    Then brain commit-check runs automatically after the commit
    77→    And task "abc123" status is set to "done"
    78→```
    79→
    80→## US-5: GitHub processor sets done on push
    81→
    82→```gherkin
    83→Feature: GitHub commit processor sets done on push to non-main branch
    84→
    85→  Scenario: Push to feature branch with task refs
    86→    Given a commit pushed to branch "feature/login" with message "task:abc123"
    87→    When the GitHub commit processor runs
    88→    Then task "abc123" status is set to "done"
    89→    And an implemented_by relation is created
    90→
    91→  Scenario: Push to main branch
    92→    Given a commit pushed to branch "main" with message "task:abc123"
    93→    When the GitHub commit processor runs
    94→    Then task "abc123" status is set to "completed"
    95→    And an implemented_by relation is created
    96→```
    97→
    98→## US-6: GitHub processor sets completed on merge to main
    99→
   100→```gherkin
   101→Feature: GitHub commit processor sets completed on merge
   102→
   103→  Scenario: PR merged to main with task refs
   104→    Given commits merged to "main" referencing "task:abc123"
   105→    When the GitHub commit processor runs for the merge
   106→    Then task "abc123" status is set to "completed"
   107→
   108→  Scenario: Task already completed (idempotent)
   109→    Given a merge to main referencing "task:abc123"
   110→    And task "abc123" already has status "completed"
   111→    When the GitHub commit processor runs
   112→    Then task "abc123" status remains "completed"
   113→```
   114→
   115→## Backward transitions (unchanged, for reference)
   116→
   117→```gherkin
   118→Feature: Server owns backward transitions
   119→
   120→  Scenario: Session aborted resets task to ready
   121→    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "in_progress"
   122→    When the session is aborted
   123→    Then the task status is set to "ready"
   124→
   125→  Scenario: Session rejected resets task to ready
   126→    Given an agent session linked to a task with status "in_progress"
   127→    When the session is rejected
   128→    Then the task status is set to "ready"
   129→```
   130→

1→# Definition of Ready Checklist: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→| # | Item | Status | Evidence |
     4→|---|------|--------|----------|
     5→| 1 | User stories trace to JTBD | Pass | All 6 stories trace to jobs 1-3 in user-stories.md |
     6→| 2 | Acceptance criteria are testable | Pass | All criteria in Gherkin Given-When-Then format |
     7→| 3 | Dependencies identified | Pass | Depends on existing commit-task-refs.ts, Brain HTTP API, git hook infrastructure |
     8→| 4 | Size estimated | Pass | 4x S, 2x M — fits single iteration |
     9→| 5 | No ambiguous requirements | Pass | Ownership table in requirements.md is explicit per transition |
    10→| 6 | Edge cases covered | Pass | Idempotency, no-refs commits, already-done tasks, crash-before-start |
    11→| 7 | Non-requirements stated | Pass | No migration, no new status values |
    12→| 8 | Shared artifacts identified | Pass | extractReferencedTaskIds (reused), update_task_status MCP tool (reused), Brain HTTP client (reused) |
    13→
    14→## Implementation Order (suggested)
    15→
    16→1. **US-1 + US-2** (remove server-side transitions) — unblocks everything, smallest risk
    17→2. **US-3** (brain commit-check command) — core new functionality
    18→3. **US-4** (post-commit hook wiring) — depends on US-3
    19→4. **US-5 + US-6** (GitHub processor enhancements) — independent of US-3/4
    20→
    21→US-1+2 can ship independently. US-3+4 and US-5+6 are two parallel tracks after that.
    22→

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}
data.promptText:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

message.content: "What if commit message doesn’t include task refs? Task stays in_progress. Need convention enforcement." this logic is already covered by the github webhook commit processor endpoint. it analyzes the commit message, so we should just reuse the same logic

content.text:
Good call — `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts` is already the shared parser. The `brain commit-check` command just needs to import and reuse it. That's already stated in R3 of the requirements. No changes needed — the reuse is baked in.

Ready to proceed to `/nw:design` when you want, or do you want to adjust anything else first?

content.text:
Ah — so the GitHub commit processor has an LLM fallback that analyzes the commit message semantically when explicit `task:id` refs aren't found. That's a second-pass detection layer.

For `brain commit-check` (local post-commit hook), we should reuse the same two-pass approach:

1. **Fast path**: `extractReferencedTaskIds()` — regex, instant
2. **LLM fallback**: if no refs found, call the same LLM analysis the GitHub processor uses to infer which task(s) the commit relates to

This keeps the convention the same across local and remote paths. Let me update the requirements.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Requirements: Task Status Ownership

## Problem Statement

Task status transitions are duplicated between the server (orchestrator) and agents/processors. The server optimistically sets in_progress on assignment and done on session accept, but these are redundant with the agent's brain-start-task command and the GitHub commit processor. This creates confusion about who owns the truth and causes orphaned states on agent crashes.

## Design Principle

**Forward transitions are agent/processor-owned. Backward transitions are server-owned.**

The entity doing the work owns progress signals. The orchestrator owns recovery.

| Transition | Owner | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| → in_progress | Agent | brain-start-task command |
| → done | commit-check hook (local) / GitHub processor (remote) | git commit with task refs |
| → completed | GitHub processor | merge to main |
| → ready (abort) | Server | Session aborted |
| → ready (reject) | Server | Session rejected |

## Requirements

### R1: Remove server-side forward transitions

Remove the in_progress transition from `createAgentSession()` in `mcp-queries.ts`. The server should still link `source_session` to the task but not change status.

Remove the done transition from `acceptOrchestratorSession()` in `session-lifecycle.ts`. Session accept is a review gate, not a completion event.

### R2: Agent owns in_progress

`brain-start-task` already instructs the agent to call `update_task_status` → in_progress. This becomes the sole authority. No changes needed here.

### R3: Add `brain commit-check` CLI command

New CLI command that:
1. Reads the most recent commit message (or accepts a commit SHA)
2. Parses task refs using existing `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts`
3. Calls the Brain API to set each referenced task's status → done
4. Idempotent — setting done on an already-done task is a no-op

### R4: Wire commit-check as git post-commit hook

Install `brain commit-check` as a git post-commit hook, similar to how Brain already manages pre-commit hooks. The hook calls the Brain HTTP API.

### R5: GitHub commit processor sets done on push

Extend `github-commit-processor.ts` to set task status → done (in addition to creating `implemented_by` relations) when commits are pushed to non-main branches.

### R6: GitHub commit processor sets completed on merge to main

When commits land on the main/default branch (via merge), set referenced tasks → completed. This is the "verified/merged" signal for teams.

### R7: Keep server-owned backward transitions

Abort and reject continue to reset task status on the server side:
- Abort → ready
- Reject → ready

No changes needed here.

## Setup-Dependent Behavior

| Setup | in_progress | done | completed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solo/local | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check post-commit hook | N/A or manual |
| Team/remote | Agent (brain-start-task) | commit-check + GitHub processor | GitHub processor (merge to main) |

## Constraints

- **commit-check must not block git**: The post-commit hook must never fail the git workflow. API errors are logged but exit 0. This is a fire-and-forget signal.
- **Idempotent transitions**: Setting done on an already-done task, or completed on an already-completed task, is a no-op. Both commit-check and GitHub processor may fire for the same commit — that's fine.
- **Task ref convention required**: Completion detection depends on `task:<id>` or `tasks: <id1>, <id2>` in commit messages. No task refs = no status change. This is by design — not every commit maps to a task.

## Non-Requirements

- No migration of existing task statuses. Schema & data migration policy: breaking changes, no backfills.
- No new task status values. Existing statuses (open, todo, ready, in_progress, blocked, done, completed) are sufficient.

lines: 1. Reads the most recent commit message (or accepts a commit SHA)
-2. Parses task refs using existing `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts`
-3. Calls the Brain API to set each referenced task's status → done
-4. Idempotent — setting done on an already-done task is a no-op
+2. **Fast path**: parses task refs using existing `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `commit-task-refs.ts`
+3. **LLM fallback**: if no explicit refs found, uses the same LLM analysis as the GitHub commit processor to infer which task(s) the commit relates to
+4. Calls the Brain API to set each referenced/inferred task's status → done
+5. Idempotent — setting done on an already-done task is a no-op
### R4: Wire commit-check as git post-commit hook

content.text:
Updated R3 with the two-pass approach: regex fast path, then LLM fallback — same logic as the GitHub processor. Both local and remote paths now behave identically.

Anything else, or ready for `/nw:design`?

content.text:
# NW-DESIGN: Architecture Design

**Wave**: DESIGN (wave 3 of 6) | **Agents**: Morgan (nw-solution-architect) | **Command**: `*design-architecture`

## Overview

Execute DESIGN wave through discovery-driven architecture design. Morgan asks about business drivers and constraints first, then recommends architecture that fits. Analyzes existing codebase, evaluates open-source alternatives, produces C4 diagrams (Mermaid) as mandatory output.

## Context Files Required

- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/requirements.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/user-stories.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/domain-model.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/ux-journey.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-job-stories.md — Job stories (JTBD Phase 1)
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-four-forces.md — Four Forces analysis
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-opportunity-scores.md — Opportunity scores (if multiple jobs)
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/constraints.md — Technical and business constraints

## Discovery Flow

Architecture decisions driven by quality attributes, not pattern shopping:

### Step 1: Understand the Problem
Review JTBD artifacts from DISCUSS to understand which jobs the architecture must serve. Morgan asks: What are we building? For whom? Which quality attributes matter most? (scalability|maintainability|testability|time-to-market|fault tolerance|auditability)

### Step 2: Understand Constraints
Morgan asks: Team size/experience? Timeline? Existing systems to integrate? Regulatory requirements? Operational maturity (CI/CD, monitoring)?

### Step 3: Team Structure (Conway's Law)
Morgan asks: How many teams? Communication patterns? Does proposed architecture match org chart?

### Step 3.5: Development Paradigm Selection

Morgan identifies primary language(s) from constraints, then applies:

- **FP-native** (Haskell|F#|Scala|Clojure|Elixir): recommend Functional
- **OOP-native** (Java|C#|Go): recommend OOP
- **Multi-paradigm** (TypeScript|Kotlin|Python|Rust|Swift): present both, let user choose based on team experience and domain fit

After confirmation, ask user permission to write paradigm to project CLAUDE.md:
- FP: `This project follows the **functional programming** paradigm. Use @nw-functional-software-crafter for implementation.`
- OOP: `This project follows the **object-oriented** paradigm. Use @nw-software-crafter for implementation.`

Default if user declines/unsure: OOP. User can override later.

### Step 4: Recommend Architecture Based on Drivers
Recommend based on quality attribute priorities|constraints|paradigm from Steps 1-3.5. Default: modular monolith with dependency inversion (ports-and-adapters). Overrides require evidence.

If functional paradigm selected, Morgan adapts architectural strategy:
- Types-first design: define algebraic data types and domain models before components
- Composition pipelines: data flows through transformation chains, not method dispatch
- Pure core / effect shell: domain logic is pure, IO lives at boundaries (adapters are functions)
- Effect boundaries replace port interfaces: function signatures serve as ports
- Immutable state: state changes produce new values, no mutation in the domain
These are strategic guidance items for the architecture document — no code snippets.

### Step 5: Advanced Architecture Stress Analysis (HIDDEN -- `--residuality` flag only)
When activated: apply complexity-science-based stress analysis — stressors|attractors|residues|incidence matrix|resilience modifications. See `stress-analysis` skill.
When not activated: skip entirely, do not mention.

### Step 6: Produce Deliverables
- Architecture document with component boundaries|tech stack|integration patterns
- C4 System Context diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Container diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Component diagrams (Mermaid) -- only for complex subsystems
- ADRs for significant decisions

## Rigor Profile Integration

Before dispatching the architect agent, read rigor config from `.nwave/des-config.json` (key: `rigor`). If absent, use standard defaults.

- **`agent_model`**: Pass as `model` parameter to Task tool. If `"inherit"`, omit `model` (inherits from session).
- **`reviewer_model`**: If design review is performed, use this model for the reviewer agent. If `"skip"`, skip design review.
- **`review_enabled`**: If `false`, skip post-design review step.

## Agent Invocation

@nw-solution-architect

Execute \*design-architecture for {feature-name}.

Context files: see Context Files Required above.

**Configuration:**
- model: rigor.agent_model (omit if "inherit")
- interactive: moderate
- output_format: markdown
- diagram_format: mermaid (C4)
- stress_analysis: {true if --residuality flag, false otherwise}

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Business drivers and constraints gathered before architecture selection
- [ ] Existing system analyzed before design (codebase search performed)
- [ ] Integration points with existing components documented
- [ ] Reuse vs. new component decisions justified
- [ ] Architecture supports all business requirements
- [ ] Technology stack selected with clear rationale
- [ ] Development paradigm selected and (optionally) written to project CLAUDE.md
- [ ] Component boundaries defined with dependency-inversion compliance
- [ ] C4 System Context + Container diagrams produced (Mermaid)
- [ ] ADRs written with alternatives considered
- [ ] Handoff accepted by nw-platform-architect (DEVOP wave)

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-platform-architect (DEVOP wave)
**Deliverables**: See Morgan's handoff package specification in agent file

## Expected Outputs

```
docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/
  architecture-design.md       (includes C4 diagrams in Mermaid)
  technology-stack.md
  component-boundaries.md
  data-models.md
docs/adrs/
  ADR-NNN-*.md
CLAUDE.md (project root)   (optional: ## Development Paradigm section)
```

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

content.content: Launching skill: nw:design

content.text:
# NW-DESIGN: Architecture Design

**Wave**: DESIGN (wave 3 of 6) | **Agents**: Morgan (nw-solution-architect) | **Command**: `*design-architecture`

## Overview

Execute DESIGN wave through discovery-driven architecture design. Morgan asks about business drivers and constraints first, then recommends architecture that fits. Analyzes existing codebase, evaluates open-source alternatives, produces C4 diagrams (Mermaid) as mandatory output.

## Context Files Required

- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/requirements.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/user-stories.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/domain-model.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/ux-journey.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-job-stories.md — Job stories (JTBD Phase 1)
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-four-forces.md — Four Forces analysis
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/jtbd-opportunity-scores.md — Opportunity scores (if multiple jobs)
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/constraints.md — Technical and business constraints

## Discovery Flow

Architecture decisions driven by quality attributes, not pattern shopping:

### Step 1: Understand the Problem
Review JTBD artifacts from DISCUSS to understand which jobs the architecture must serve. Morgan asks: What are we building? For whom? Which quality attributes matter most? (scalability|maintainability|testability|time-to-market|fault tolerance|auditability)

### Step 2: Understand Constraints
Morgan asks: Team size/experience? Timeline? Existing systems to integrate? Regulatory requirements? Operational maturity (CI/CD, monitoring)?

### Step 3: Team Structure (Conway's Law)
Morgan asks: How many teams? Communication patterns? Does proposed architecture match org chart?

### Step 3.5: Development Paradigm Selection

Morgan identifies primary language(s) from constraints, then applies:

- **FP-native** (Haskell|F#|Scala|Clojure|Elixir): recommend Functional
- **OOP-native** (Java|C#|Go): recommend OOP
- **Multi-paradigm** (TypeScript|Kotlin|Python|Rust|Swift): present both, let user choose based on team experience and domain fit

After confirmation, ask user permission to write paradigm to project CLAUDE.md:
- FP: `This project follows the **functional programming** paradigm. Use @nw-functional-software-crafter for implementation.`
- OOP: `This project follows the **object-oriented** paradigm. Use @nw-software-crafter for implementation.`

Default if user declines/unsure: OOP. User can override later.

### Step 4: Recommend Architecture Based on Drivers
Recommend based on quality attribute priorities|constraints|paradigm from Steps 1-3.5. Default: modular monolith with dependency inversion (ports-and-adapters). Overrides require evidence.

If functional paradigm selected, Morgan adapts architectural strategy:
- Types-first design: define algebraic data types and domain models before components
- Composition pipelines: data flows through transformation chains, not method dispatch
- Pure core / effect shell: domain logic is pure, IO lives at boundaries (adapters are functions)
- Effect boundaries replace port interfaces: function signatures serve as ports
- Immutable state: state changes produce new values, no mutation in the domain
These are strategic guidance items for the architecture document — no code snippets.

### Step 5: Advanced Architecture Stress Analysis (HIDDEN -- `--residuality` flag only)
When activated: apply complexity-science-based stress analysis — stressors|attractors|residues|incidence matrix|resilience modifications. See `stress-analysis` skill.
When not activated: skip entirely, do not mention.

### Step 6: Produce Deliverables
- Architecture document with component boundaries|tech stack|integration patterns
- C4 System Context diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Container diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Component diagrams (Mermaid) -- only for complex subsystems
- ADRs for significant decisions

## Rigor Profile Integration

Before dispatching the architect agent, read rigor config from `.nwave/des-config.json` (key: `rigor`). If absent, use standard defaults.

- **`agent_model`**: Pass as `model` parameter to Task tool. If `"inherit"`, omit `model` (inherits from session).
- **`reviewer_model`**: If design review is performed, use this model for the reviewer agent. If `"skip"`, skip design review.
- **`review_enabled`**: If `false`, skip post-design review step.

## Agent Invocation

@nw-solution-architect

Execute \*design-architecture for {feature-name}.

Context files: see Context Files Required above.

**Configuration:**
- model: rigor.agent_model (omit if "inherit")
- interactive: moderate
- output_format: markdown
- diagram_format: mermaid (C4)
- stress_analysis: {true if --residuality flag, false otherwise}

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Business drivers and constraints gathered before architecture selection
- [ ] Existing system analyzed before design (codebase search performed)
- [ ] Integration points with existing components documented
- [ ] Reuse vs. new component decisions justified
- [ ] Architecture supports all business requirements
- [ ] Technology stack selected with clear rationale
- [ ] Development paradigm selected and (optionally) written to project CLAUDE.md
- [ ] Component boundaries defined with dependency-inversion compliance
- [ ] C4 System Context + Container diagrams produced (Mermaid)
- [ ] ADRs written with alternatives considered
- [ ] Handoff accepted by nw-platform-architect (DEVOP wave)

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-platform-architect (DEVOP wave)
**Deliverables**: See Morgan's handoff package specification in agent file

## Expected Outputs

```
docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/
  architecture-design.md       (includes C4 diagrams in Mermaid)
  technology-stack.md
  component-boundaries.md
  data-models.md
docs/adrs/
  ADR-NNN-*.md
CLAUDE.md (project root)   (optional: ## Development Paradigm section)
```

input.prompt:
Execute *design-architecture for task-status-ownership.

## Context

This is a backend refactoring of task status lifecycle ownership in an existing system. The project is a TypeScript/Bun application with SurrealDB, using a functional programming paradigm (already set in CLAUDE.md).

### Requirements Summary
Forward task transitions (in_progress, done, completed) move from server-owned to agent/processor-owned. Backward transitions (abort, reject → ready) stay server-owned.

| Transition | Owner | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| → in_progress | Agent | brain-start-task command |
| → done | commit-check hook (local) / GitHub processor (remote) | git commit with task refs |
| → completed | GitHub processor | merge to main |
| → ready (abort) | Server | Session aborted |
| → ready (reject) | Server | Session rejected |

### User Stories (6 total)
- US-1: Remove server-side in_progress from createAgentSession()
- US-2: Remove server-side done from acceptOrchestratorSession()  
- US-3: Add brain commit-check CLI command (two-pass: regex fast path, then LLM fallback)
- US-4: Wire commit-check as git post-commit hook
- US-5: GitHub processor sets done on push to non-main branches
- US-6: GitHub processor sets completed on merge to main

### Key Constraints
- commit-check must not block git (fire-and-forget, exit 0 on errors)
- Idempotent transitions (both commit-check and GitHub processor may fire for same commit)
- Functional programming paradigm
- Existing codebase uses Bun, TypeScript, SurrealDB, Vercel AI SDK

### Existing Code Locations
- `app/src/server/mcp/mcp-queries.ts` - createAgentSession() with in_progress transition (lines 546-561)
- `app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts` - acceptOrchestratorSession() with done transition (lines 557-562), abortOrchestratorSession() and rejectOrchestratorSession() with backward transitions
- `app/src/server/webhook/github-commit-processor.ts` - processCommit() creates implemented_by relations
- `app/src/server/webhook/commit-task-refs.ts` - extractReferencedTaskIds() regex parser
- `cli/commands/init-content.ts` - brain-start-task slash command
- `cli/mcp-server.ts` - update_task_status MCP tool
- `cli/http-client.ts` - Brain HTTP client

### Requirements files
- docs/requirements/task-status-ownership/requirements.md
- docs/requirements/task-status-ownership/user-stories.md
- docs/requirements/task-status-ownership/acceptance-criteria.md
- docs/ux/task-status-ownership/jtbd-job-stories.md
- docs/ux/task-status-ownership/jtbd-four-forces.md
- docs/ux/task-status-ownership/jtbd-opportunity-scores.md

## Instructions

1. Read the requirements files and existing code to understand the current architecture
2. This is NOT greenfield - analyze the existing codebase first
3. Skip paradigm selection (already functional)
4. Skip Steps 1-3.5 (we've already discussed drivers/constraints in the DISCUSS wave)
5. Focus on Step 4 (architecture recommendation) and Step 6 (deliverables)
6. Produce:
   - Architecture design document with C4 diagrams (Mermaid)
   - Component boundaries showing what changes in each existing module
   - ADR for the ownership model decision
7. Write outputs to docs/design/task-status-ownership/
8. Do NOT be interactive - we've already made all decisions in the DISCUSS wave. Just produce the deliverables.

**Key architectural decisions:**

- **Reuse over reimplementation**: `extractReferencedTaskIds()` imported directly by CLI from server module (pure function, no server dependencies). `BrainHttpClient.updateTaskStatus()` already exists.

- **Existing `check-commit` renamed/extended**: The CLI already has `brain check-commit` for pre-commit analysis. The new `commit-check` adds post-commit task status updates as a separate command.

        traces: [US-5, R5]

      "03-02":
        title: "GitHub processor sets completed on merge to main"
        description: "When push targets the default branch, set referenced tasks to completed instead of done"
        acceptance_criteria:
          - "Merge/push to main with task refs sets tasks to completed"
          - "Already-completed tasks remain completed without error"
          - "Non-main branch pushes still set done (not completed)"
        architectural_constraints:
          - "Same processCommit() modification, branching on isDefaultBranch"
          - "Extend ProcessWebhookResult with taskStatusUpdates for observability"
        traces: [US-6, R6]

implementation_scope:
  files_modified:
    - path: "app/src/server/mcp/mcp-queries.ts"
      change: "Remove in_progress status promotion from createAgentSession()"
    - path: "app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts"
      change: "Remove done status update from acceptOrchestratorSession()"
    - path: "cli/commands/git-hooks.ts"
      change: "Add runCommitCheck() command"
    - path: "cli/brain.ts"
      change: "Add commit-check routing case"
    - path: "cli/commands/init.ts"
      change: "Add post-commit hook installation"
    - path: "app/src/server/webhook/github-commit-processor.ts"
      change: "Add status transitions (done/completed) based on branch"
    - path: "app/src/server/webhook/types.ts"
      change: "Extend ProcessWebhookResult with taskStatusUpdates"

  files_unchanged:
    - path: "app/src/server/webhook/commit-task-refs.ts"
      reason: "Pure function reused as-is"
    - path: "cli/http-client.ts"
      reason: "updateTaskStatus() already exists"
    - path: ".claude/commands/brain-start-task.md"
      reason: "Already instructs agent to call update_task_status"
    - path: "app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts (abort/reject)"
      reason: "Backward transitions unchanged"

validation:
  quality_gates:
    - "All forward transitions removed from server (US-1, US-2)"
    - "commit-check never blocks git workflow"
    - "Idempotent transitions: duplicate fires produce no error"
    - "Backward transitions (abort/reject -> ready) unchanged"
    - "Existing smoke test extended for webhook status transitions"
