1→# Architecture Design: Task Status Ownership
     2→
     3→## System Context
     4→
     5→Task status lifecycle transitions are currently split between the server (orchestrator) and agents/processors, creating ambiguity about source of truth. This refactoring moves **forward transitions** to the entity doing the work (agent, commit hook, GitHub processor) while keeping **backward transitions** (abort/reject) server-owned.
     6→
     7→### Capabilities
     8→
     9→| Capability | Owner | Mechanism |
    10→|---|---|---|
    11→| in_progress | Agent | `brain-start-task` slash command calls `update_task_status` MCP tool |
    12→| done | commit-check CLI (local) / GitHub processor (remote) | Task refs parsed from commit messages |
    13→| completed | GitHub processor | Merge to main/default branch detected |
    14→| ready (abort) | Server | `abortOrchestratorSession()` |
    15→| ready (reject) | Server | `rejectOrchestratorSession()` |
    16→
    17→### Setup-Dependent Behavior
    18→
    19→| Setup | in_progress | done | completed |
    20→|---|---|---|---|
    21→| Solo/local | Agent | commit-check post-commit hook | N/A or manual |
    22→| Team/remote | Agent | commit-check + GitHub processor | GitHub processor (merge) |
    23→
    24→## C4 System Context (L1)
    25→
    26→```mermaid
    27→graph TB
    28→    dev[Developer]
    29→    lead[Team Lead]
    30→    claude[Claude Code Agent]
    31→    github[GitHub]
    32→    brain[Brain Platform]
    33→
    34→    dev -->|post-commit hook| brain
    35→    dev -->|git push| github
    36→    claude -->|MCP: in_progress| brain
    37→    github -->|Webhook: done/completed| brain
    38→    lead -->|Web UI| brain
    39→```
    40→
    41→## C4 Container (L2)
    42→
    43→```mermaid
    44→graph TB
    45→    dev[Developer]
    46→    github[GitHub]
    47→    claude[Claude Code Agent]
    48→
    49→    subgraph brain [Brain Platform]
    50→        cli[Brain CLI - Bun binary]
    51→        server[Brain Server - Bun HTTP]
    52→        surreal[(SurrealDB v3)]
    53→    end
    54→
    55→    claude -->|MCP stdio| cli
    56→    cli -->|HTTP POST| server
    57→    dev -->|post-commit hook| cli
    58→    github -->|Webhook POST| server
    59→    server -->|SurrealDB SDK| surreal
    60→```
    61→
    62→## C4 Component (L3) -- Status Transition Flow
    63→
    64→```mermaid
    65→graph TB
    66→    claude[Claude Agent]
    67→    github[GitHub]
    68→    dev[Developer]
    69→
    70→    subgraph cli_boundary [Brain CLI]
    71→        post_commit_hook[post-commit hook]
    72→        commit_check[commit-check command]
    73→        mcp_server[MCP Server]
    74→    end
    75→
    76→    subgraph server_boundary [Brain Server]
    77→        mcp_route[MCP Route]
    78→        session_lifecycle[Session Lifecycle - abort/reject]
    79→        create_session[createAgentSession - NO status change]
    80→        webhook_handler[Webhook Handler]
    81→        commit_processor[Commit Processor - done/completed]
    82→        task_ref_parser[commit-task-refs - pure function]
    83→    end
    84→
    85→    claude -->|MCP| mcp_server
    86→    mcp_server -->|HTTP POST /tasks/status| mcp_route
    87→    dev -->|git commit| post_commit_hook
    88→    post_commit_hook -->|shell exec| commit_check
    89→    commit_check -->|HTTP POST /tasks/status| mcp_route
    90→    github -->|Webhook POST| webhook_handler
    91→    webhook_handler -->|async| commit_processor
    92→    commit_processor --> task_ref_parser
    93→    session_lifecycle -.->|removed| create_session
    94→```
    95→
    96→## Component Architecture
    97→
    98→### Changes by Module
    99→
   100→#### 1. `app/src/server/mcp/mcp-queries.ts` -- createAgentSession()
   101→- **Remove**: Lines 546-561 conditional `status: "in_progress"` update
   102→- **Keep**: `source_session` linkage to task record
   103→- **Keep**: Workspace scope validation
   104→- **Traces to**: US-1, R1
   105→
   106→#### 2. `app/src/server/orchestrator/session-lifecycle.ts` -- acceptOrchestratorSession()
   107→- **Remove**: Lines 557-563 `status: "done"` update on task
   108→- **Keep**: `orchestrator_status: "completed"` on session
   109→- **Keep**: Agent session end, handle registry cleanup
   110→- **Traces to**: US-2, R1
   111→
   112→#### 3. `cli/commands/git-hooks.ts` -- NEW: runCommitCheck()
   113→- **Add**: New `brain commit-check` command — thin client
   114→- **Behavior**: Read latest commit message, POST it to server endpoint, exit 0
   115→- **Constraint**: Fire-and-forget, exit 0 on any error. No parsing or LLM logic in CLI.
   116→- **Traces to**: US-3, R3
   117→
   118→#### 3a. `POST /api/mcp/:workspaceId/commits/check` -- NEW server endpoint
   119→- Accepts a commit message (and optional SHA) from the CLI
   120→- **Two-pass task detection** (all server-side):
   121→  1. **Fast path**: `extractReferencedTaskIds()` (regex, instant)
   122→  2. **LLM fallback**: If no explicit refs found, runs `extractStructuredGraph()` (same extraction pipeline as the GitHub commit processor) to infer task matches
   123→- **For each matched task**: Sets status → done (idempotent)
   124→- **Reuses**: `extractReferencedTaskIds()` from `webhook/commit-task-refs.ts`, `extractStructuredGraph()` from `extraction/extract-graph.ts`, extraction model from `dependencies.ts`
   125→- **Traces to**: US-3, R3
   126→
   127→#### 4. `cli/commands/init.ts` -- Post-commit hook installation
   128→- **Add**: Install `brain commit-check` as `.git/hooks/post-commit` alongside existing pre-commit hook
   129→- **Script**: `#!/bin/sh\nbrain commit-check\n` (fire-and-forget, no error check)
   130→- **Traces to**: US-4, R4
   131→
   132→#### 5. `cli/brain.ts` -- CLI routing
   133→- **Add**: `commit-check` case (currently only `check-commit` exists; rename or add alias)
   134→- **Traces to**: US-3, US-4
   135→
   136→#### 6. `app/src/server/webhook/github-commit-processor.ts` -- processCommit()
   137→- **Add**: After creating `implemented_by` relations, set task status based on branch:
   138→  - Non-default branch push: set referenced tasks to `done`
   139→  - Default branch (main) push: set referenced tasks to `completed`
   140→- **Input**: Branch info available from `GitHubPushEvent.ref` and `GitHubPushEvent.repository.default_branch`
   141→- **Constraint**: Idempotent -- skip if task already at target status or beyond
   142→- **Traces to**: US-5, US-6, R5, R6
   143→
   144→#### 7. `app/src/server/webhook/github-webhook-route.ts` -- handleGitHubWebhook()
   145→- **Add**: Pass `event.ref` and `event.repository.default_branch` through to `processGitCommits()` (already available in `event`)
   146→- **Traces to**: US-5, US-6
   147→
   148→#### 8. `app/src/server/webhook/types.ts` -- ProcessWebhookResult
   149→- **Add**: `taskStatusUpdates: Array<{ taskId: string; newStatus: string }>` to result types for observability
   150→- **Traces to**: US-5, US-6
   151→
   152→### Shared Module: commit-task-refs.ts
   153→
   154→The `extractReferencedTaskIds()` function in `app/src/server/webhook/commit-task-refs.ts` is already a pure function with no server dependencies. It can be imported directly by the CLI commit-check command since both CLI and server run on Bun.
   155→
   156→**No duplication needed.** The CLI imports the server module directly at build time (CLI is compiled to a binary via `bun build`).
   157→
   158→### Idempotent Status Transitions
   159→
   160→Status transitions follow a forward-only ordering: `open < todo < ready < in_progress < done < completed`. A transition request is a no-op if the task is already at or beyond the target status. This is enforced at the API layer (`updateTaskStatus` MCP route) rather than duplicated across callers.
   161→
   162→### Transition Ordering (conflict resolution)
   163→
   164→| Scenario | commit-check fires | GitHub processor fires | Result |
   165→|---|---|---|---|
   166→| Local commit, then push | Sets done | Sets done (no-op) | done |
   167→| Push to feature branch | May not run (remote CI) | Sets done | done |
   168→| Merge to main | N/A | Sets completed | completed |
   169→| Commit on main directly | Sets done | Sets completed (upgrades) | completed |
   170→
   171→## Technology Stack
   172→
   173→No new technology introduced. All changes use existing stack:
   174→
   175→| Component | Technology | License | Rationale |
   176→|---|---|---|---|
   177→| CLI | Bun + TypeScript | MIT | Existing CLI runtime |
   178→| Server | Bun HTTP | MIT | Existing server runtime |
   179→| Database | SurrealDB v3 | BSL 1.1 | Existing data store |
   180→| Task ref parsing | Regex (existing) | N/A | `extractReferencedTaskIds()` already exists |
   181→| HTTP client | Fetch API (existing) | N/A | `BrainHttpClient.updateTaskStatus()` already exists |
   182→
   183→## Integration Patterns
   184→
   185→### CLI to Server (commit-check)
   186→- **Protocol**: HTTP POST to `/api/mcp/{workspaceId}/tasks/status`
   187→- **Auth**: Bearer token from `~/.brain/config.json` (existing OAuth2 flow)
   188→- **Payload**: `{ task_id: string, status: "done" }`
   189→- **Error handling**: Fire-and-forget. Log to stderr, exit 0.
   190→
   191→### GitHub to Server (webhook)
   192→- **Protocol**: HTTP POST webhook (existing)
   193→- **Trigger**: Push event (existing handler)
   194→- **Branch detection**: Compare `event.ref` against `refs/heads/${event.repository.default_branch}`
   195→- **Status mapping**: default branch = `completed`, other branches = `done`
   196→
   197→### Agent to Server (in_progress)
   198→- **Protocol**: MCP stdio (existing)
   199→- **Tool**: `update_task_status` (existing, no changes)
   200→- **Trigger**: `brain-start-task` slash command (existing, no changes)
   201→
   202→## Quality Attribute Strategies
   203→
   204→### Reliability
   205→- **Idempotent transitions**: Forward-only status ordering prevents conflicts between commit-check and GitHub processor
   206→- **Fire-and-forget hook**: Post-commit hook never blocks git workflow
   207→- **Backward transitions preserved**: Server-owned abort/reject continue unchanged
   208→
   209→### Maintainability
   210→- **Single ownership per transition**: Each status change has exactly one authoritative source
   211→- **Shared pure functions**: `extractReferencedTaskIds()` reused without duplication
   212→- **Existing API reuse**: `BrainHttpClient.updateTaskStatus()` already exists
   213→
   214→### Observability
   215→- **Logging**: Each transition logs source (commit-check, webhook, agent) with task ID and commit SHA
   216→- **Result types**: `ProcessWebhookResult` extended with `taskStatusUpdates` for tracking
   217→
   218→### Testability
   219→- **Pure function**: Task ref parsing is already testable in isolation
   220→- **HTTP boundary**: commit-check tests mock `BrainHttpClient`
   221→- **Webhook tests**: Existing smoke test (`tests/smoke/github-webhook.test.ts`) extended for status transitions
   222→
   223→## Deployment Architecture
   224→
   225→No deployment changes. The CLI binary is rebuilt with `bun build` (existing process). Server deploys unchanged. Post-commit hook is installed via `brain init` (existing mechanism, extended).
   226→

1→---
     2→name: bdd-methodology
     3→description: BDD patterns for acceptance test design - Given-When-Then structure, scenario writing rules, pytest-bdd implementation, anti-patterns, and living documentation
     4→---
     5→
     6→# BDD Methodology for Acceptance Test Design
     7→
     8→## Core Philosophy
     9→
    10→Test units of behavior, not units of code. Acceptance tests validate business outcomes through public interfaces, decoupled from implementation.
    11→
    12→## Outside-In Double-Loop TDD
    13→
    14→The acceptance-designer creates the outer loop of Outside-In TDD. Development starts from user perspective, drives inward.
    15→
    16→**Outer loop (acceptance/BDD)**: Hours to days | User perspective, business language | Defines "done" | Scenarios describe user goals and observable outcomes, not internals | Failing outer-loop test is the starting signal for implementation
    17→
    18→**Inner loop (unit/TDD)**: Minutes | Developer perspective, technical terms | Software-crafter owns this loop
    19→
    20→Workflow:
    21→1. Write failing acceptance test from user perspective (outer loop -- outside)
    22→2. Software-crafter drops to inner loop: unit tests to implement components (inside)
    23→3. Iterate inner loop until acceptance test passes
    24→4. Passing acceptance test proves user value delivered
    25→5. Repeat for next behavior
    26→
    27→Outer loop defines WHAT users need (outside). Inner loop drives HOW to build it (inside).
    28→
    29→## Given-When-Then Structure
    30→
    31→```gherkin
    32→Scenario: [Business-focused title describing one behavior]
    33→  Given [preconditions - system state in business terms]
    34→  When [single user action or business event]
    35→  Then [observable business outcome]
    36→```
    37→
    38→### Scenario Writing Rules
    39→
    40→**Rule 1: One scenario, one behavior** -- Split multi-behavior scenarios.
    41→
    42→**Rule 2: Declarative, not imperative** -- Business outcomes, not UI interactions. "When I log in with valid credentials" not "When I click Login button and enter email."
    43→
    44→**Rule 3: Concrete examples, not abstractions** -- "Given my account balance is $100.00" not "Given the user has sufficient funds."
    45→
    46→**Rule 4: Keep scenarios short (3-5 steps)** -- Longer means testing multiple behaviors or irrelevant details.
    47→
    48→**Rule 5: Background for shared Given steps only** -- Only Given steps. Actions/validations in scenarios.
    49→
    50→## Scenario Categorization
    51→
    52→- **Happy path**: Primary successful workflows
    53→- **Error path**: Invalid inputs, failures, unauthorized access (target 40%+ of scenarios)
    54→- **Edge case**: Boundary conditions, unusual but valid behavior
    55→- **Integration**: Cross-component/system interactions
    56→
    57→### Golden Path + Key Alternatives
    58→
    59→Per capability: 1. Happy path (most common success) | 2. Alternative paths (valid but less common) | 3. Error paths (invalid inputs, constraint violations). Select representative examples revealing different business rules. Do not test every combination.
    60→
    61→### Scenario Outlines for Boundary Testing
    62→
    63→```gherkin
    64→Scenario Outline: Account minimum balance validation
    65→  Given I have an account with balance $<initial_balance>
    66→  When I attempt to withdraw $<withdrawal_amount>
    67→  Then the withdrawal is <result>
    68→
    69→  Examples: Valid withdrawals
    70→    | initial_balance | withdrawal_amount | result   |
    71→    | 100.00         | 50.00            | accepted |
    72→    | 25.00          | 25.00            | accepted |
    73→
    74→  Examples: Invalid withdrawals
    75→    | initial_balance | withdrawal_amount | result                       |
    76→    | 100.00         | 101.00           | rejected (insufficient funds) |
    77→```
    78→
    79→Use outlines for boundary conditions and calculation variations. Avoid when scenarios diverge structurally.
    80→
    81→## pytest-bdd Implementation
    82→
    83→### Step Definitions with Fixture Injection
    84→
    85→```python
    86→from pytest_bdd import scenarios, given, when, then, parsers
    87→
    88→scenarios('../features/account.feature')
    89→
    90→@given("I am authenticated", target_fixture="authenticated_user")
    91→def authenticated_user(auth_service):
    92→    user = auth_service.create_and_authenticate("test@example.com")
    93→    return user
    94→
    95→@given(parsers.parse('my account balance is ${amount:g}'),
    96→       target_fixture="account")
    97→def account_with_balance(authenticated_user, account_service, amount):
    98→    return account_service.create_account(authenticated_user, balance=amount)
    99→```
   100→
   101→### Step Organization by Domain
   102→
   103→Organize by domain concept, not feature file:
   104→```
   105→steps/
   106→  authentication_steps.py  # All auth-related steps
   107→  account_steps.py         # All account-related steps
   108→  transaction_steps.py     # All transaction-related steps
   109→```
   110→
   111→### Fixture Scopes for Performance
   112→Session: expensive setup (DB engine, app instance) | Module: schema creation | Function: data cleanup (autouse=True)
   113→
   114→### Production-Like Test Environment
   115→
   116→```python
   117→@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
   118→def app():
   119→    """Application instance with production-like configuration."""
   120→    app = create_app({"environment": "test", "database": "postgresql://localhost/test_db"})
   121→    with app.app_context():
   122→        app.db.create_all()
   123→    yield app
   124→    with app.app_context():
   125→        app.db.drop_all()
   126→```
   127→
   128→Use real services (database, message queue) with test data. Avoid mocks at acceptance level.
   129→
   130→## Anti-Patterns
   131→
   132→| Anti-Pattern | Fix |
   133→|-------------|-----|
   134→| Testing through UI | Test through service/API layer |
   135→| Multiple WHEN actions | Split into separate scenarios |
   136→| Feature-coupled steps | Organize by domain concept |
   137→| Conjunction steps ("Given A and B" as one step) | Break into atomic steps |
   138→| Incidental details | Include only behavior-relevant info |
   139→| Technical jargon in scenarios | Business domain language |
   140→| Abstract scenarios | Concrete values, specific examples |
   141→| Rambling scenarios (8+ steps) | Extract to 3-5 focused steps |
   142→
   143→## Living Documentation
   144→
   145→Scenarios serve dual purpose: executable tests and living documentation. Organization: Business Goal > Capability > Feature > Scenario > Test. Each scenario traces to business capability. Stakeholders see which capabilities are implemented, tested, passing.
   146→
   147→### Documentation-Grade Scenarios
   148→Replace HTTP verbs with business actions, JSON with domain concepts, status codes with business outcomes. Add context about WHO and WHY.
   149→

1→---
     2→name: test-design-mandates
     3→description: Three design mandates for acceptance tests - hexagonal boundary enforcement, business language abstraction, user journey completeness, and walking skeleton strategy
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Acceptance Test Design Mandates
     7→
     8→Three mandates enforced during peer review. All must pass before handoff to software-crafter.
     9→
    10→## Mandate 1: Hexagonal Boundary Enforcement
    11→
    12→Tests invoke through driving ports (entry points), never internal components.
    13→
    14→### Driving Ports (Test Through These)
    15→Application services/orchestrators | API controllers/CLI handlers | Message consumers/event handlers | Public API facade classes
    16→
    17→### Not Entry Points (Never Test Directly)
    18→Internal validators, parsers, formatters | Domain entities/value objects | Repository implementations | Internal service components
    19→
    20→### Correct Pattern
    21→
    22→```python
    23→# Invoke through system entry point (driving port)
    24→from myapp.orchestrator import AppOrchestrator
    25→
    26→def when_user_performs_action(self):
    27→    orchestrator = AppOrchestrator()
    28→    self.result = orchestrator.perform_action(
    29→        context=self.context
    30→    )
    31→```
    32→
    33→### Violation Pattern
    34→
    35→```python
    36→# Invoking internal component directly
    37→from myapp.validator import InputValidator  # INTERNAL
    38→
    39→def when_user_validates_input(self):
    40→    validator = InputValidator()  # WRONG BOUNDARY
    41→    self.result = validator.validate(self.input)
    42→```
    43→
    44→Testing internal components creates Testing Theater: tests pass but users cannot access feature through actual entry point. Integration wiring bugs remain hidden.
    45→
    46→## Mandate 2: Business Language Abstraction
    47→
    48→Step methods speak business language, abstract all technical details.
    49→
    50→### Three Abstraction Layers
    51→
    52→**Layer 1 - Gherkin**: Pure business language, all stakeholders. Domain terms from ubiquitous language | Zero technical jargon | Describe WHAT user does, not HOW system does it
    53→
    54→```gherkin
    55→Scenario: Customer places order for available product
    56→  Given customer has items in shopping cart
    57→  When customer submits order
    58→  Then order is confirmed
    59→  And customer receives confirmation email
    60→```
    61→
    62→**Layer 2 - Step Methods**: Business service delegation. Method names use domain terms | Delegate to business service layer (OrderService, not HTTP client) | Assert business outcomes (order.is_confirmed()), not technical state (status_code == 201)
    63→
    64→```python
    65→def when_customer_submits_order(self):
    66→    self.result = self.order_service.place_order(
    67→        customer=self.customer, items=self.cart_items
    68→    )
    69→
    70→def then_order_is_confirmed(self):
    71→    assert self.result.is_confirmed()
    72→    assert self.result.has_order_number()
    73→```
    74→
    75→**Layer 3 - Business Services**: Production services handle technical implementation. HTTP calls, DB transactions, SMTP hidden inside service layer.
    76→
    77→### Test Smell Indicators
    78→`requests.post()` in step method | `db.execute()` in step method | `assert response.status_code` | Technical terms in Gherkin
    79→
    80→## Mandate 3: User Journey Completeness
    81→
    82→Tests validate complete user journeys with business value, not isolated technical operations.
    83→
    84→### Complete Journey Structure
    85→Every scenario includes: **User trigger** (Given/When) | **Business logic** (When - system processes rules) | **Observable outcome** (Then - user sees result) | **Business value** (Then - value delivered)
    86→
    87→### Correct Example
    88→
    89→```gherkin
    90→Scenario: Customer successfully completes purchase
    91→  Given customer has selected products worth $150
    92→  And customer has valid payment method
    93→  When customer submits order
    94→  Then order is confirmed with order number
    95→  And customer receives email confirmation
    96→  And order appears in customer's order history
    97→```
    98→
    99→### Violation Example
   100→
   101→```gherkin
   102→Scenario: Order validator accepts valid order data
   103→  Given valid order JSON exists
   104→  When validator.validate() is called
   105→  Then validation passes
   106→# Tests isolated validation, not user journey
   107→```
   108→
   109→### Scenario Name Test
   110→Does name express user value or technical operation? "Customer completes purchase" = correct. "Validator accepts JSON" = violation.
   111→
   112→## Walking Skeleton Strategy
   113→
   114→Balance user-centric E2E integration tests with focused boundary tests.
   115→
   116→### Walking Skeletons (2-5 per feature)
   117→Trace thin vertical slice delivering observable user value E2E | Each answers: "Can a user accomplish this goal and see the result?" | Express simplest complete user journey | Validate system delivers demo-able stakeholder value | Touch all layers as consequence of journey, not as design goal
   118→
   119→### Walking Skeleton Litmus Test
   120→1. Title describes user goal ("Customer purchases a product") not technical flow ("Order passes through all layers")
   121→2. Given/When describe user actions/context, not system state setup
   122→3. Then describe user observations (confirmation, email, receipt), not internal side effects (DB row, message queued)
   123→4. Non-technical stakeholder can confirm "yes, that is what users need"
   124→
   125→### Focused Scenarios (15-20 per feature, majority)
   126→Test specific business rules at driving port boundary | Test doubles for external dependencies (faster, isolated) | Cover business rule variations and edge cases | Invoke through entry point (OrderService, Orchestrator)
   127→
   128→### Recommended Ratio
   129→For typical feature with 20 scenarios: 2-3 walking skeletons (user value E2E) | 17-18 focused scenarios (boundary tests with test doubles). Walking skeletons prove users achieve goals. Focused scenarios run fast, cover breadth. Both use business language and invoke through entry points.
   130→
   131→## Mandate Compliance Verification
   132→
   133→Handoff to software-crafter includes proof all three mandates pass:
   134→- **CM-A**: All test files import entry points (driving ports), zero internal component imports
   135→- **CM-B**: Gherkin uses business terms only, step methods delegate to services
   136→- **CM-C**: Scenarios validate complete user journeys with business value
   137→
   138→Evidence: import listings, grep for technical terms, walking skeleton identification, focused scenario count.
   139→

1→---
     2→name: critique-dimensions
     3→description: Review dimensions for acceptance test quality - happy path bias, GWT compliance, business language purity, coverage completeness, walking skeleton user-centricity, and priority validation
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Acceptance Test Critique Dimensions
     7→
     8→Load when performing peer review of acceptance tests (during *handoff-develop).
     9→
    10→## Dimension 1: Happy Path Bias
    11→
    12→**Pattern**: Only successful scenarios, error paths missing.
    13→
    14→Detection: Count success vs error scenarios. Error should be at least 40%. Missing coverage examples: login success but no invalid password | Payment processed but no decline/timeout | Search results but no empty/error cases.
    15→
    16→Severity: blocker (production error handling untested).
    17→
    18→## Dimension 2: GWT Format Compliance
    19→
    20→**Pattern**: Scenarios violate Given-When-Then structure.
    21→
    22→Violations: Missing Given context | Multiple When actions (split into separate scenarios) | Then with technical assertions instead of business outcomes. Each scenario: Given (context), When (single action), Then (observable outcome).
    23→
    24→Severity: high (tests not behavior-driven).
    25→
    26→## Dimension 3: Business Language Purity
    27→
    28→**Pattern**: Technical terms leak into acceptance tests.
    29→
    30→Flag: database, API, HTTP, REST, JSON, classes, methods, services, controllers, status codes (500, 404), infrastructure (Redis, Kafka, Lambda).
    31→
    32→Business alternatives: "Customer data is stored" not "Database persists record" | "Order is confirmed" not "API returns 200 OK" | "Payment fails" not "Gateway throws exception"
    33→
    34→Severity: high (tests coupled to implementation).
    35→
    36→## Dimension 4: Coverage Completeness
    37→
    38→**Pattern**: User stories lack acceptance test coverage.
    39→
    40→Validation: Map each story to scenarios | Verify all AC have corresponding tests | Confirm edge cases and boundaries tested.
    41→
    42→Severity: blocker (unverified requirements).
    43→
    44→## Dimension 5: Walking Skeleton User-Centricity
    45→
    46→**Pattern**: Walking skeletons describe technical layer connectivity instead of user value.
    47→
    48→Detection litmus test for `@walking_skeleton` scenarios:
    49→- Title describes user goal or technical flow?
    50→- Then steps describe user observations or internal side effects?
    51→- Could non-technical stakeholder confirm "yes, that is what users need"?
    52→
    53→Violations: "End-to-end order flow through all layers" (technical framing) | Then "order row inserted in database" (internal side effects) | Given "database contains user record" instead of "customer has an account"
    54→
    55→Severity: high (skeletons that only prove wiring miss the point -- first skeleton should be demo-able to stakeholder).
    56→
    57→## Dimension 6: Priority Validation
    58→
    59→**Pattern**: Tests address secondary concerns while larger gaps exist.
    60→
    61→Questions: 1. Is this the largest bottleneck? (timing data or gap analysis) | 2. Simpler alternatives considered? | 3. Constraint prioritization correct? | 4. Test design decisions data-justified?
    62→
    63→Severity: blocker if wrong problem addressed, high if no measurement data.
    64→
    65→## Review Output Format
    66→
    67→```yaml
    68→review_id: "accept_rev_{timestamp}"
    69→reviewer: "acceptance-designer (review mode)"
    70→
    71→strengths:
    72→  - "{positive test design aspect with example}"
    73→
    74→issues_identified:
    75→  happy_path_bias:
    76→    - issue: "Feature {name} only tests success"
    77→      severity: "blocker"
    78→      recommendation: "Add error scenarios: invalid input, timeout, service failure"
    79→
    80→  gwt_format:
    81→    - issue: "Scenario has multiple When actions"
    82→      severity: "high"
    83→      recommendation: "Split into separate scenarios"
    84→
    85→  business_language:
    86→    - issue: "Technical term '{term}' in scenario"
    87→      severity: "high"
    88→      recommendation: "Replace with: '{business alternative}'"
    89→
    90→  coverage_gaps:
    91→    - issue: "User story {US-ID} has no acceptance tests"
    92→      severity: "blocker"
    93→      recommendation: "Create scenarios for all AC of {US-ID}"
    94→
    95→  walking_skeleton_centricity:
    96→    - issue: "Walking skeleton '{name}' describes technical flow, not user goal"
    97→      severity: "high"
    98→      recommendation: "Reframe: title as user goal, Then steps as observable user outcomes"
    99→
   100→approval_status: "approved|rejected_pending_revisions|conditionally_approved"
   101→```
   102→

input.content:
# Test Scenarios: Task Status Ownership

## Summary

27 total scenarios across 4 test files. 14 unit tests (pure function, no server). 13 smoke tests (real server + SurrealDB).

Error/edge path ratio: 12/27 = 44% (exceeds 40% target).

## Walking Skeletons (3)

These prove the core user value end-to-end:

1. **Session creation preserves task status** (smoke) -- Creating an agent session for a task no longer promotes status to in_progress. Validates US-1.
2. **Commit with task ref sets task to done** (smoke) -- The commit-check endpoint parses a task ref from a commit message and sets the task to done. Validates US-3.
3. **Push to feature branch sets task done, merge to main sets completed** (smoke) -- GitHub webhook push to feature branch sets done; push to main sets completed. Validates US-5 + US-6.

## Test Files

### Unit: `tests/unit/task-status-ownership/extract-task-refs.test.ts`

Pure function tests for `extractReferencedTaskIds()`. No server needed.

| # | Scenario | Type | US |
|---|----------|------|----|
| 1 | Single task:id extracted | happy | US-3 |
| 2 | Task ref at start of message | happy | US-3 |
| 3 | Task ref embedded mid-sentence | happy | US-3 |
| 4 | tasks: list extracts all ids | happy | US-3 |
| 5 | Both task: and tasks: formats, deduplication | happy | US-3 |
| 6 | Multiple task: tokens in one message | happy | US-3 |
| 7 | No task refs returns empty list | error | US-3 |
| 8 | Non-id words after tasks: prefix | error | US-3 |
| 9 | Very short tokens rejected | edge | US-3 |
| 10 | Duplicate refs deduplicated | edge | US-3 |
| 11 | Token without digits rejected | edge | US-3 |
| 12 | Hyphens and digits accepted | edge | US-3 |
| 13 | Underscores accepted | edge | US-3 |

(Note: test #14 is count including the 1 implicit: total 13 tests in this file)

### Smoke: `tests/smoke/task-status-ownership/session-no-status-change.test.ts`

Server integration tests for US-1 and US-2.

| # | Scenario | Type | US |
|---|----------|------|----|
| 1 | Ready task remains ready after session creation | happy (skeleton) | US-1 |
| 2 | Todo task remains todo after session creation | happy | US-1 |
| 3 | In_progress task remains in_progress after session creation | edge | US-1 |
| 4 | In_progress task remains in_progress after session accept | happy (skeleton) | US-2 |
| 5 | Done task remains done after session accept | edge | US-2 |

### Smoke: `tests/smoke/task-status-ownership/commit-check-endpoint.test.ts`

Server integration tests for the commit-check endpoint (US-3).

| # | Scenario | Type | US |
|---|----------|------|----|
| 1 | Single task ref sets task to done | happy (skeleton) | US-3 |
| 2 | Multiple task refs set all tasks to done | happy | US-3 |
| 3 | No task refs leaves tasks unchanged | error | US-3 |
| 4 | Already-done task remains done (idempotent) | error | US-3 |
| 5 | Nonexistent task ref handled gracefully | error | US-3 |
| 6 | Missing commit message returns validation error | error | US-3 |
| 7 | Invalid workspace returns 404 | error | US-3 |

### Smoke: `tests/smoke/task-status-ownership/webhook-status-transitions.test.ts`

Server integration tests for GitHub webhook status transitions (US-5, US-6).

| # | Scenario | Type | US |
|---|----------|------|----|
| 1 | Push to feature branch sets task to done + creates implemented_by | happy (skeleton) | US-5 |
| 2 | Already-done task remains done on feature branch push (idempotent) | error | US-5 |
| 3 | Push to main sets task to completed | happy (skeleton) | US-6 |
| 4 | In_progress task goes to completed on main push | edge | US-6 |
| 5 | Already-completed task remains completed on main push (idempotent) | error | US-6 |
| 6 | Feature branch done -> main push upgrades to completed | happy | US-5+6 |
| 7 | Push without task refs does not change task status | error | US-5 |

### Smoke: `tests/smoke/task-status-ownership/backward-transitions.test.ts`

Server integration tests for backward transitions (unchanged behavior).

| # | Scenario | Type | US |
|---|----------|------|----|
| 1 | Abort resets in_progress task to ready | happy | R7 |
| 2 | Reject resets in_progress task to ready | happy | R7 |
| 3 | Abort resets done task to ready | edge | R7 |

## Coverage Matrix

| User Story | Scenarios | Walking Skeleton |
|------------|-----------|------------------|
| US-1: Remove server in_progress on assignment | 3 | session-no-status-change #1 |
| US-2: Remove server done on session accept | 2 | session-no-status-change #4 |
| US-3: brain commit-check | 13 unit + 7 smoke | commit-check-endpoint #1 |
| US-4: Wire as post-commit hook | (see note) | -- |
| US-5: GitHub done on push | 2 | webhook-status-transitions #1 |
| US-6: GitHub completed on merge | 4 | webhook-status-transitions #3 |
| R7: Backward transitions | 3 | -- |

**US-4 note**: Post-commit hook wiring is a shell script integration. It is tested by verifying `brain init` creates the hook file. This is covered by the existing `tests/unit/cli-init.test.ts` pattern -- a new test should be added there when the hook installation code lands.

## Implementation Sequence

All smoke tests are marked `it.skip`. Enable one at a time as implementation proceeds:

1. **Enable** unit tests in `extract-task-refs.test.ts` -- these should pass immediately (existing function)
2. **Enable** `session-no-status-change.test.ts` scenario 1 -- implement US-1 (remove in_progress from createAgentSession)
3. **Enable** `session-no-status-change.test.ts` scenario 4 -- implement US-2 (remove done from acceptOrchestratorSession)
4. **Enable** `commit-check-endpoint.test.ts` scenario 1 -- implement US-3 (server endpoint)
5. **Enable** `webhook-status-transitions.test.ts` scenario 1 -- implement US-5 (webhook done on feature branch)
6. **Enable** `webhook-status-transitions.test.ts` scenario 3 -- implement US-6 (webhook completed on main)
7. **Enable** remaining scenarios for each US after the walking skeleton passes
8. **Enable** `backward-transitions.test.ts` -- verify R7 unchanged behavior

## Mandate Compliance Evidence

**CM-A (Driving port enforcement)**: All smoke tests invoke through HTTP endpoints (the driving ports). No internal function imports in smoke tests. Unit tests import only the pure `extractReferencedTaskIds` function.

**CM-B (Business language purity)**: Test descriptions use domain terms: "task status", "session creation", "commit referencing", "push to feature branch", "merge to main". No HTTP verbs, status codes, or JSON structure in test names.

**CM-C (Walking skeleton + focused scenario counts)**: 3 walking skeletons + 24 focused scenarios across 4 files. Walking skeletons are stakeholder-demo-able ("can I see that creating a session no longer changes my task status?").
